author: @tehspamgozehere
text:
Oh I love the dome nut trick. I'm used to having to find a suitably small washer to act as a spacer, possibly even going to the trouble of getting a larger washer and turning it down smaller on the lathe. The dome nut though acts as both fastener and spacer. I'll have to remember that one.

The guy on the Primitive Technology channel (I think) has a neat trick for keeping a roller centered. If the rails it is meant to run on are say six inches apart, make the axle ten inches, and toward the ends of the axle make the diameter slightly smaller. When the rolling object drifts to one side, the other side drops down to the narrower axle which turns it just a little slower which steers the load gently the other way and thus corrects itself. As a simple self-correcting travelling load on an axle, it's an elegant solution.  I can't help but wonder if there isn't something similar we could do with the rollers and cylinder-chamber here to correct drift.

I've intended to make mine sit up on a slight angle to keep pulling the material down the slope toward the bottom of the chamber and just have a pair of narrow roller wheels there to cushion it, but that involves more moving parts and thus points of wear and possible failure.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
I use a cement mixer, and even that moves almost too fast at 90 RPM [granted the diameter is much larger] so I have room for large heavy grinding media for coarse work and medium or small media, as the job requires.

And, in between times, it also helps chip off excess hardened to keep the mixer clean! (~_^)-b [just kidding! I wash my machine between loads of concrete to prevent build up and I am sure you would never want cement dust contaminating your product unless you had already carefully calculated it and planned for it!]

replies.author: @macgyver5108
replies.text: With something as _big_ as a cement mixer why not kill two birds with one stone and just do a load of laundry to clean it? (A load of _WORK clothes_ of course, not your "Sunday's best"!😁)

text: You could use a dimmer for an ac motor but you'd need a PWM for a dc. My next vid will be a while as I have to travel for work for the next four weeks

Or would it have to be a proper motor controller? Many thanks for sharing and you now have me intrigued by what your going to make with this mill. Looking forward to your next video.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: You can make graphene oxide with it if you put graphite an dry ice in there

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: A whole range of very interesting things. For example aluminium graphite composites the are piezoelectric, doped graphenes like nickel hydroxide doped graphene ( which is what I want it for) just hundreds of things
